title: US-China Chip Battle Intensifies as Nvidia CEO Joins Trump in Beijing
subhead: The Netherlands pushes back on US export control legislation while approved Nvidia chip sales to China stall amid Beijing's reluctance. Europe moves to ban Huawei from 6G networks, and critical infrastructure faces escalating cyber threats.

sections.heading: Nvidia's China Dilemma: Approved Sales, No Deliveries
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has approved around 10 Chinese companies including Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance and JD.com to purchase Nvidia's H200 chips, with each buyer permitted up to 75,000 chips under US licensing terms. Yet despite approval, not a single delivery has been made. Chinese firms are pulling back after guidance from Beijing, which fears imports could weaken its push to develop homegrown AI chips.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang joined President Trump's delegation to Beijing at the last minute, seeking a breakthrough. Before US export curbs tightened, Nvidia commanded about 95% of China's advanced chip market; China once accounted for 13% of its revenue. Huang has warned that US export controls are eroding the company's foothold, saying its share of AI accelerators in China has effectively fallen to zero.

The path to completed sales faces obstacles on both sides. US rules require Chinese buyers to install 'sufficient security procedures' and pledge non-military use. The Trump administration negotiated a 25% revenue share for the US on chip sales—requiring chips to pass through US territory before shipment to China, since US law bars direct export fees. Beijing worries about potential tampering or hidden vulnerabilities.
sections.whyItMatters: The standoff reveals a new phase in the US-China tech rivalry: even approved trade is stalling. China is prioritizing domestic chip development over imports, even for superior technology. Nvidia risks permanent market loss while Chinese AI firms like DeepSeek increasingly rely on domestic alternatives.

sections.heading: Dutch Government Objects to US MATCH Act Targeting ASML
sections.body:
The Netherlands has formally objected to proposed US legislation that could further restrict exports to China by Dutch chipmaking equipment maker ASML. The MATCH Act, advancing through Congress with a 36-8 committee vote, would coerce allied countries into tighter export controls—or face US sanctions. It targets critical technology from the US, Japan, and the Netherlands, the three countries that dominate advanced semiconductor equipment.

ASML, the world's dominant supplier of deep ultraviolet immersion lithography, would face new restrictions on its China market. The company has raised its 2026 guidance as AI semiconductor demand stays strong, reflecting continued orders even amid geopolitical pressure.

China has responded aggressively to the proposed legislation, preparing countermeasures including a 'Malicious Entity List' for those who promote improper extraterritorial measures. The Chinese Ministry of Commerce called US actions 'unilateral bullying' that 'seriously undermine the legitimate rights and interests of Chinese businesses.' A 90-day suspension of retaliatory measures against US companies was announced following Geneva trade talks, but the underlying tensions remain unresolved.
sections.whyItMatters: The Netherlands' objection signals allied fatigue with US extraterritorial pressure. ASML is caught between American demands and European commercial interests. If the MATCH Act passes, it could force a fundamental restructuring of the global semiconductor equipment market.

sections.heading: TSMC Raises Global Chip Market Forecast to $1.5 Trillion
sections.body:
TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, now expects the global semiconductor market to exceed $1.5 trillion by 2030—a 50% upgrade from its previous $1 trillion forecast. The Taiwan-based foundry cited surging AI demand as the primary driver, with the company outlining rapid capacity expansion including nine new wafer fab and packaging phases across multiple countries.

The forecast reflects the structural transformation of the chip industry. AI accelerators and high-performance computing now dominate growth projections, pushing foundries to expand aggressively. TSMC's advanced node capacity remains central to the AI supply chain, with major chip designers still dependent on its manufacturing capabilities.

However, the expansion comes amid escalating geopolitical risk. TSMC's concentration in Taiwan remains a strategic vulnerability for the global tech industry, with ongoing tensions between the US and China over chip sovereignty and export controls directly affecting the company's customer base.
sections.whyItMatters: The upgraded forecast confirms AI's transformation from niche application to industrial backbone. TSMC's expansion plans reveal where capital is flowing—but Taiwan's geopolitical position means the entire AI supply chain remains exposed to cross-strait risk.

sections.heading: Europe Moves to Exclude Huawei from 6G Infrastructure
sections.body:
The European Commission has reissued its recommendation that member states exclude Huawei and ZTE from connectivity infrastructure—now broadening beyond 5G to include fixed networks, fiber-optic cables, undersea cables, and satellite networks. More significantly, the restrictions are moving toward becoming legally binding through a draft cybersecurity law presented in January 2026.

Under the proposed law, components from designated high-risk suppliers would have to be removed within 36 months of rules taking effect, with infringement procedures and financial penalties for non-compliant member states. Only 13 of 27 EU members had implemented concrete 5G security measures by early 2026, prompting Brussels to conclude that 'it didn't work on a voluntary basis.'

Germany remains the largest holdout, with Huawei equipment estimated in approximately 60% of German 5G sites. The cost and complexity of replacement has slowed Berlin's response. China has threatened retaliation against European companies operating in its market—an asymmetric threat given EU exposure in autos, luxury goods, and machinery.

New German Chancellor Friedrich Merz has stated the country won't allow Chinese components in its 6G network, signaling a harder line from Europe's largest economy.
sections.whyItMatters: The shift from voluntary recommendation to binding law marks a turning point in European digital sovereignty policy. If implemented, the 36-month phase-out would reshape European telecom infrastructure and likely trigger Chinese countermeasures against EU firms.

sections.heading: CISA Launches CI Fortify as Infrastructure Attacks Escalate
sections.body:
The US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency has launched CI Fortify, a program helping critical infrastructure operators defend against state-sponsored hackers during geopolitical conflicts. The initiative assumes that in any conflict scenario, 'third-party connections—telecommunications, internet, vendors, service providers—will be unreliable' and that 'threat actors will have some access to the [operational technology] network.'

The program focuses on isolation and recovery planning—acknowledging that US officials assess China is already burrowed into non-military critical infrastructure to enable sabotage during a Taiwan conflict. Earlier this year, US intelligence agencies assessed that hackers linked to China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea continue to pose critical threats to US infrastructure.

Separately, Sweden publicly attributed a destructive cyberattack attempt on a thermal power plant to hackers with 'connections to Russian intelligence and security services.' The attack in early 2025 was blocked by a built-in protection mechanism, but Swedish officials warned that pro-Russian groups 'are now attempting destructive cyber attacks against organizations in Europe'—a shift from earlier denial-of-service attacks.

International cyber agencies also issued warnings about China-linked hackers using everyday devices—home routers, smart devices—to build covert networks for concealing malicious activity. The guidance, coordinated by Britain's NCSC with partners from nine countries, highlighted attacks on critical sectors globally.
sections.whyItMatters: Critical infrastructure cyber defense has shifted from theoretical to operational. Governments are now planning for inevitable breaches during conflicts, not trying to prevent them entirely. The Swedish and US initiatives reflect an acceptance that state-sponsored actors are pre-positioned inside critical systems.

sections.heading: Europe's AI Sovereignty Illusion
sections.body:
Europe is pouring €20 billion into AI gigafactories as part of a broader €200 billion InvestAI initiative. Yet analysis reveals a fundamental constraint: GPUaaS expands access to compute, but does not change who controls it. US hyperscalers—AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud—control roughly 70% of European cloud infrastructure revenue. Nvidia holds about 85% of the AI GPU segment, and Europe has 'no chance of chip independence' at either the design or manufacturing layer in the short term.

The combined CapEx for Google, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft on AI infrastructure in 2026 is approximately $725 billion—up 77% from $410 billion in 2025—exceeding the GDP of many European countries. European spending on sovereign cloud infrastructure is forecast to reach only €12 billion in 2026, an order of magnitude below US hyperscaler investment.

Europe's 14 supercomputers and 19 AI Factories under the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking, backed by roughly €10 billion in funding through 2027, still depend on GPUs designed by Nvidia and AMD, manufactured by TSMC. The analysis concludes that 'access is not sovereignty'—and that Europe's investments risk deepening dependence on US hyperscalers rather than reducing it.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's AI infrastructure spending may accelerate dependency rather than resolve it. Without domestic GPU design or advanced manufacturing capability, €20 billion in gigafactories could lock Europe into US-controlled supply chains for the critical compute layer of AI development.

sections.heading: India-Russia Critical Minerals Partnership Advances
sections.body:
India and Russia are expanding cooperation on lithium, rare earth elements, and permanent magnet manufacturing as both countries seek to reduce dependence on Chinese supply chains. The proposed framework covers the full mineral value chain: exploration, extraction, refining, and advanced materials processing.

India possesses reserves of rare earth-bearing minerals, including monazite deposits, but relies heavily on imports for advanced processing and high-performance permanent magnets such as neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB)—essential for electric vehicles, wind turbines, and defense technologies. Russian institutions and companies involved in rare earth research and nuclear materials processing are viewed as potential technology partners.

The partnership reflects broader global concerns about supply chain concentration. China currently dominates rare earth refining and magnet manufacturing, prompting India to explore overseas lithium opportunities in South America and Africa as part of its energy transition strategy.
sections.whyItMatters: Alternative critical mineral supply chains are forming outside Western alliances. The India-Russia partnership targets the same strategic vulnerability as US and EU initiatives—but through a different geopolitical alignment. This diversification could reduce China's leverage but creates new dependencies on Russia.

bottomLine: The US-China tech rivalry has entered a new phase: even approved trade is stalling as Beijing prioritizes domestic chip development. Nvidia's approved H200 sales remain undelivered, the Netherlands is resisting US pressure on ASML, and Europe is moving from voluntary recommendations to binding law on Huawei exclusion. Meanwhile, TSMC's $1.5 trillion forecast confirms AI's structural growth—but critical infrastructure cyber defense has shifted from prevention to breach acceptance, and alternative supply chains are forming through non-Western partnerships.
sources: Reuters, US News & World Report, China Daily, The Next Web, TechCrunch, Gizmodo, Domain-B, CISA, NCSC, Bloomberg, MSN, Forbes, Yahoo Finance, Chatham House
